The '''BRC-TZ''' ''BCR-TZ'' (Buseleum Reconnaisance Consortium-Type Z; [[Aurielleno]]: Buseleum Cowensorcio Resorase-Tuwis Z) is a Auriellen 5.56×45mm NATO assault rifle developed in the mid 2000s by the state-owned small arms research and development manufacturer Buseleum Reconnaisance Consortium based in Nereneavos. The weapon was successfully trialed between 2006 and 2007 and approved for serial production in 2009 at Vallis Descando in [[Level 618]]. The BRC-TZ was intended to replace the HK33 as the standard service weapon of the Auriellen Ground Forces; however, it was considered too ambitious and expensive an undertaking, and thus it was ultimately decided that it would be placed in service alongside the other [[The Frontrooms|Frontrooms]]-derived weapons. In 2010, the first rifles were delivered, by which time orders for approximately 7,000 had been placed. From 2012, the BRC-TZ would be a weapon of choice and would become the de facto standard service rifle of the General Aerial Operations Command's air assault units. ==Development== The Auriellen Armed Forces rejected proposals to widely implement the stockpile of largely unused Kalashnikov rifles, which had been acquired from both [[Entity 25|Counter Entities]] and the liberation of a large amount of Auriellen territories by the end of the Leosaros Offensive, as the weapons used across all branches of the military were mainly chambered for 5.56×45mm to 7.62×51mm NATO ammunition, leaving little place for the 7.62×39mm without significantly complicating logistics. Nonetheless, it was acknowledged that they could be reutilized and hence the Federal Ministry of Defense directed the Armed Forces Logistical Directorate to procure a suitable replacement for the Kalashnikovs capable of using 5.56×45mm NATO ammunition but still retaining the reliable internal components of the Soviet rifle family. The state-owned Buseleum Reconnaisance Consortium was placed in charge of developing the rifle. Between 2004 and 2005, two principles drove the conception of the design: similarities with rifles from Heckler & Koch to take advantage of the fact that many soldiers used the Frontrooms company's rifles and the ability to handle the specifications underlined per the Armed Forces Logistical Directorate's request. The BRC-TX prototype model was developed based on the [https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/CETME_Model_L CETME Model L] and the [https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/FN_FNC FN FNC]. The design principles and bases that guided its creation would also be retained in the subsequent BRC-TZ model, albeit with some improvements. The BRC-TX design was briefly tested by the Armed Forces Logistical Directorate before being rushed into combat. While its performance was average, a few critical issues were noted while being combat tested, especially regarding heat from sustained fire: *The handguard did not fully encompass the barrel, allowing users to be easily injured by the hot metal. *The radiating heat from the barrel could block the sight picture after sustained fire. *The rifle was heavier than expected. Following combat tests, the Buseleum Reconnaissance Consortium made further iterations to the rifle classified under the BRC-TY. The handguard was reinforced and modified to fully encompass the barrel, utilizing a thicker mold for enhanced insulation. Properly treated metals were employed in the receiver, with stamped steel used for the upper portion and aluminum alloys for the lower. Additionally, a thicker barrel profile was implemented. To reduce weight, the old stock was replaced with a foldable, skeletonized design, and some components were substituted with plastics. Few additional changes to the BRC-TY, such as a more modern sighting system and improved plastics, due to reports of overheating, were introduced and compounded the christening of the weapon as the BRC-TZ. The BRC-TZ passed trials from 2006 to 2007 and was then approved for serial production in 2009. ==Design Details== ===Operating mechanism=== The BRC-TZ is a selective-fire rifle that uses a gas-operated long-stroke piston system and a rotating bolt locking mechanism equipped with two locking lugs that engage corresponding recesses in the barrel extension. The build-up of gases inside the gas cylinder drives the interaction of the piston and bolt carrier, alongside the ejector, which rotates the bolt and unlocks it from the barrel via a camming pin. The rear part of the cocking handle slot, cut in the upper receiver for the cocking handle, is covered by a spring-loaded cover which automatically opens by the handle when it goes back and automatically closes the opening when the cocking handle returns forward. ===Features=== The spring extractor is located on the right-hand locking lug of the bolt, and the ejector is fixed and is generally riveted or welded to the receiver housing. The weapon is hammer-fired and has a fire control lever, which is also the manual safety. Both the safety and fire selector are located on the left side of the receiver, directly above the grip, and have three settings: "S" (Securitate; safety)—the weapon's safety, whose trigger is disabled mechanically; "1"—semi-automatic fire; and "A" (Awtomicus; automatic)—automatic fire. The BRC-TZ fires from a closed bolt position and uses the NATO-standard 5.56×45mm cartridge. The BRC-TZ's barrel features a "chipped" flash suppressor. Some models of the gun could mount a knife-type bayonet, but it was mostly phased out to cut costs. The BRC-TZ's stock, at least the fixed version, grip, and handguard, are made of a combination of lightweight plastics. ===Feeding=== The rifle feeds from standard NATO magazines that adheres to STANAG-DRAFT 4179 to allow with cross compability with other popular rifles in the Ground Forces, such as the M16, and had a capacity of 30 rounds, but it can also use short 20 and 12-round box magazines. Buseleum had several problems when trying to adapt the 5.56×45mm to the Kalashnikov's workings, which resulted in several problems, like misfeeds, stuck magazines, and the like (continuing to this day but at a somewhat lesser scale). Per regulations, it was forbidden to discard the BRC-TZ's magazines, and many soldiers were punished for losing them. Auriellen soldiers at times attempted to keep better-fitted magazines from surplus stores or friends, which were said to fix the issue. ===Sights=== The BRC-TZ is equipped with adjustable iron sights, having two versions each with their specific roles. The thin one was usually preferred over the thick one due to costing less and being better for precision shooting, even if it bent when hit. The rear sight has two apertures with settings for 250 m (273 yd) and 400 m (437 yd), while the front sight post is adjustable for elevation. Optics, such as the Cascadan JTND and KQ-12 sights, can be attached with the use of a receiver-mounted adapter. ===Accessories=== Standard equipment supplied with the rifle includes a cleaning kit, sling, bayonet, lightweight bipod, rifle grenade launcher guide, and magazine loader tool. ==Variants== The BRC-TZ generally only had one configuration until a short (carbine) model was made to avoid wasting excess resources on rifles used by units stationed in levels that present indoor environments. It also allowed for better performance in close-quarters combat and moving vehicles. It came at the expense of losing the ability to attach a grenade launcher. While the BRC-TZ is afflicted by the regulations of the Auriellen veteran system, which stipulate that the soldiers keep their own personal equipment, including personally assigned weapons, at home or in an armory, it is often considered an outlier, and soldiers are unable to keep the rifle. Alongside this, Buseleum does not officially sell the BRC-TZ for civilians, although some civilian gunsmiths offer replicas that oblige gun regulations.
